subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — United States v. Eichman (1990)
stimulus:
After the Supreme Court held that a state law in Texas that criminalized the desecration of the American flag was unconstitutional, Congress passed a national law, the Flag Protection Act of 1989, which criminalized the conduct of anyone who "knowingly mutilates, defaces, physically defiles, burns, maintains on the floor or ground, or tramples upon" a flag of the United States.

In response to this law, protest demonstrations took place in several locations across the country. Several people, including Shawn Eichman, burned flags on the steps of the United States Capitol building in Washington, D.C., before a crowd of reporters and photographers. Eichman and others were arrested for violating the Flag Protection Act.

In the ensuing cases, Eichman and others defended their actions as legitimate forms of political protest. Federal district judges dismissed the charges, but United States attorneys appealed the decisions directly to the Supreme Court.

In United States v. Eichman (1990), the Supreme Court's 5–4 decision held that the federal government cannot prosecute a person for desecration of the American flag because doing so would violate the First Amendment.

parts.prompt: Identify the civil liberty that is common to both Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) and United States v. Eichman (1990).
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'freedom of speech,' 'free speech,' 'First Amendment speech rights,' or 'freedom of expression.' Only First Amendment speech rights earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the civil liberty that is common to both Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) and United States v. Eichman (1990).

required_elements: The civil liberty is freedom of speech or freedom of expression.
alternatives.correct_example: The civil liberty is freedom of speech or freedom of expression.

parts.prompt: Explain how the reasoning in Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission and United States v. Eichman led to a similar holding in both cases.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate description of Citizens United reasoning. Second point requires connecting the reasoning in BOTH cases to explain the similar holdings — both involving protected First Amendment expression.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the reasoning in Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission and United States v. Eichman led to a similar holding in both cases. (1 point — describe relevant fact/holding about the required Supreme Court case)

required_elements: In Citizens United, the reasoning of the court led it to hold that political advertisements and communications were a protected form of free speech.
alternatives.correct_example: In Citizens United, the reasoning of the court led it to hold that political advertisements and communications were a protected form of free speech.

common_traps: Describing only one case without explaining the comparison does not earn the 2-point tier

scoring_points.description: Explain how the reasoning in Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission and United States v. Eichman led to a similar holding in both cases. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different/similar holdings)

required_elements: In Citizens United, the reasoning of the court led it to hold that political advertisements and communications were a protected form of free speech. In Eichman, the court reasoned that burning a flag in protest was symbolic speech. In both cases, the court held that the action was a form of speech that is protected by the First Amendment.
alternatives.correct_example: In Citizens United, the reasoning of the court led it to hold that political advertisements and communications were a protected form of free speech. In Eichman, the court reasoned that burning a flag in protest was symbolic speech. In both cases, the court held that the action was a form of speech that is protected by the First Amendment.

common_traps: Describing only one case earns 1 point, not 2
Must connect facts to different outcomes

parts.prompt: Explain how those unhappy with the precedent established in Eichman can use a constitutional process to have it overturned.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must identify a specific constitutional process — the amendment process (Article V). Accept either method of proposing amendments (congressional proposal or constitutional convention) combined with ratification.

scoring_points.description: Explain how those unhappy with the precedent established in Eichman can use a constitutional process to have it overturned.

required_elements: States can ratify an amendment that would invalidate the decision.
alternatives.correct_example: States can ratify an amendment that would invalidate the decision.

required_elements: A constitutional convention can be called to propose an amendment that would invalidate the decision and then state constitutional conventions can ratify it. Total for question 3
alternatives.correct_example: A constitutional convention can be called to propose an amendment that would invalidate the decision and then state constitutional conventions can ratify it. Total for question 3
